1: Psalm 11 is a psalm of trust in the face of counsel to flee. David's counselors or friends advise escape to the mountains — presumably because the danger is too acute to face. The "mountain" (hārekem) is probably the Judean highlands (not Zion), a refuge for fugitives in the David narratives (1 Sam 23-26). David's refusal — "In the LORD I take refuge" — is the psalm's theological claim: the divine protector supersedes any geographic strategy. The tension between the counselors' pragmatic wisdom and the psalmist's covenantal trust is the pastoral core of the poem.
2: "The wicked string the bow; they have set their arrows on the string to shoot in the darkness (ʾōpel)" — the nocturnal archer was a real threat in the ancient world, but the image also captures hidden malice: the attack comes without warning, in darkness, targeting "the upright in heart (yiśrê-lēb)." Upright-hearted people are the ones who cannot be corrupted by the counselors' pragmatic flight advice — their very integrity makes them targets. ANE military contexts regularly described ambush arrows as the weapon of treachery.
3: "If the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?" — the counselors' logic: if the basic structures of justice and social order have collapsed, individual righteousness has no purchase; flight is the only rational option. Šātôt (foundations) can mean the foundations of society, the covenant order, or the temple mount. Second Temple readers in the aftermath of the Babylonian destruction of 587 BCE would have heard this as directly relevant — what does covenant faithfulness mean when every institutional support of that covenant has been razed?
4: "The LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD's throne is in heaven" — the counter-claim to despair. Two levels: the earthly temple (where YHWH is present) and the heavenly throne (from which he governs). Both are secure and neither depends on the survival of human institutions. "His eyes observe; his eyelids examine (yiḥĕzû, yiblĕnû) humanity" — the examining gaze is forensic. Bāḥan (examine/test) is the metallurgist's assaying verb: God is scrutinizing humanity as a refiner tests metal. He is neither absent nor indifferent.
5: "The LORD tests (bōḥēn) the righteous" — the divine testing of the righteous is a covenant theme: God tests to refine (Deuteronomy 8:2-3; Job 1-2; Psalms 66:10; Zechariah 13:9). "His soul hates the wicked and the one who loves violence (ḥāmās)" — ḥāmās (violence, cruelty) is one of the most weighty moral terms in the Hebrew Bible. It is listed alongside corruption as the reason for the flood (Genesis 6:11-13) and is the defining characteristic of the wicked throughout the Psalter. God's hatred of ḥāmās is not emotional pique but the covenantal rejection of the anti-creation force.
6: "Let him rain down coals on the wicked; fire and sulfur and a scorching wind (rûaḥ zilʿāpôt)" — Sodom imagery (Genesis 19:24) is deliberate: fire and sulfur raining from heaven is the paradigmatic divine judgment on the utterly wicked city. The scorching wind is likely the sirocco (east wind from the Arabian desert), which withers vegetation and exhausts humans. Fire from above, sulfur from below, scorching wind from the east — judgment from all directions simultaneously. "Their cup (mĕnāt kôsām)" — cup of wrath contrasts sharply with the cup of blessing in Psalms 16:5.
7: "For the LORD is righteous (ṣaddîq); he loves righteous deeds (ṣĕdāqôt); the upright will see his face (yĕḥĕzû pānāyw)" — the closing affirmation. To "see God's face" was the goal of temple worship (Psalms 17:15; Psalms 27:4; Psalms 63:2) — the culmination of access to the divine presence normally mediated through the sanctuary. The upright's access to the divine presence is grounded in the correspondence between their character and YHWH's own character: as he is righteous, the righteous see him. The psalm reverses the flight-counsel of Psalms 11:1: those who trust rather than flee are the ones who arrive at the divine presence.
